metadata.title: The Data Contract: What Your Inputs Actually Mean
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: Part 3 (12 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 3 of 7. Part 3 is the data contract: twelve points for translating your A3 sources from file formats into human meaning. Here is the distinction that matters. In A3, you described your data as file types and record counts — a CSV with 1,200 rows, a JSON feed, a YouTube API response. That is the technical description. Part 3 asks the strategic question: what does this data represent for the problem you are solving? Not the schema — the meaning. And then: how do you know the data is good enough to build on? That checkable standard — not 'the data is good' — is the quality gate that Part 4 will use. This episode covers what each field in the data contract means, shows you a weak version and a strong version for each, and ends with a prompt that converts your A3 inventory into a complete Part 3 in one pass.

props.segment: The Data Contract: What Your Inputs Actually Mean
props.command: Translate each A3 data source from a file format into its meaning for the user's problem — then write a checkable quality standard for each.
props.runningText: Part 3 converts A3 technical descriptions into strategic meaning — this becomes brand_config.json in 5B…
output: What the data IS: file type, record count, update frequency
What it REPRESENTS: plain-English meaning for the user's problem
How we know it's good: a checkable quality standard — not 'the data is good'

beats.narration_text: The first field in the data contract is what the data IS — the technical description. File type. Record count or volume estimate. Update frequency. Where it lives locally. This field is the one you can pull directly from A3 — you already have it. NewsAPI JSON feed, updated hourly, approximately 150 articles per day, saved to data/raw/newsapi/. Kaggle CSV, 50,000 rows, static historical dataset collected between 2021 and 2024, saved to data/raw/kaggle/sentiment.csv. YouTube Data API response, 500 videos per query, updated on each workflow run. That is the 'what it IS' field for each source. The important thing here is the local path: every source entry ends with the path in your repository where the saved file lives. This is not metadata — it is the field that Part 5B and your brand_config.json will read to find the data. Get the path right.

props.artifactHeading: Technical description — pull directly from A3
artifactLines: File type + record count + update frequency + local path — four fields.
Example: 'NewsAPI JSON feed · ~150 articles/day · updates hourly · data/raw/newsapi/'
Example: 'Kaggle CSV · 50,000 rows · static 2021–2024 · data/raw/kaggle/sentiment.csv'
Example: 'YouTube Data API · 500 videos/query · updates each workflow run'
The local path matters — 5B and brand_config.json will read it. Get it right.
props.sparkLine: You already have this from A3. Pull it.

beats.narration_text: The second field is what the data represents — and this is where most students fail Part 3. They describe the file again in slightly different words. 'This CSV contains tweet text, timestamps, and sentiment scores' is still a description of the file. What the data represents asks: what does this mean for the user's problem? For the same sentiment CSV: 'This dataset represents how public sentiment about [brand topic] shifted over three years — giving the user a baseline for how opinion moved before the tool existed, so they can compare current performance against a pre-AI reference point.' That is what the data represents for the user. The signal is: if a non-technical person read your 'what it represents' sentence, would they understand why this data exists in the tool? If they would still ask 'but what is it for?', you have described the file, not the meaning.

props.artifactHeading: Plain-English meaning — not the schema again
artifactLines: Wrong: 'This CSV contains tweet text, timestamps, and sentiment scores.' That's the file description again.
Right: 'This dataset represents how public sentiment about [topic] shifted over 3 years — a baseline to compare current performance against a pre-AI reference point.'
Test: if a non-technical person reads this sentence, do they understand WHY this data is in the tool?
If they still ask 'but what is it for?' — you have described the file, not the meaning.
Connect every source back to the user's problem statement from Part 1 and 2A.
props.sparkLine: What the data means for the user — not what the file contains.

beats.narration_text: The third field is the quality standard — how you know the data is good enough to build on. This is the field most students skip or write wrong. 'The data is good' is not a quality standard. 'The data is clean' is not a quality standard. A quality standard is a checkable rule — something you can run a script against, something a grader could verify, something you could write as a passing or failing test. Examples that work: 'Every record has a date field in ISO 8601 format, a source URL, and a text body of at least 20 words.' 'No column exceeds 5 percent null values.' 'The date range covers at least six months ending no earlier than three months before submission.' These are checkable. They become your validation gate in Part 4. They are what GIGO means in practice — garbage in has a definition; you either meet the standard or you don't. Write one standard per source. One standard. Specific. Checkable.

props.artifactHeading: A checkable rule — not 'the data is clean'
artifactLines: Wrong: 'The data is good.' Wrong: 'The data is clean.' Not checkable.
Right: 'Every record has a date in ISO 8601, a source URL, and ≥20 words of body text.'
Right: 'No column exceeds 5% null values.'
Right: 'Date range covers ≥6 months ending no earlier than 3 months before submission.'
One standard per source. Specific. Checkable. This becomes your Part 4 validation gate.
GIGO means garbage has a definition. Write the definition.
props.sparkLine: Checkable means you could write a failing test for it.

beats.narration_text: A note on where Part 3 goes next. In Assignment 5B, you build a brand configuration file — brand_config.json — that the tool reads to know what it is and what it works with. Part 3 of the Conductor Brief is the written specification that drives that config file. The data sources, their local paths, their quality standards — all of that becomes structured fields in brand_config.json. Writing Part 3 carefully now means you are not rewriting it in 5B. The same structure applies: source name, what it IS, what it represents, quality standard, local path. If you write those five fields clearly in Part 3, the config file almost writes itself. The connection to make explicit in your brief: each data contract entry maps directly to a config entry. If a source is in the contract but not in the config, the tool does not know it exists. If it is in the config but not the contract, you have data without a rationale.

artifactLines: In 5B, brand_config.json tells the tool what data it has and where to find it.
Part 3's five fields per source map directly: name · IS · represents · quality standard · local path.
Writing Part 3 carefully now means not rewriting it in 5B.
Rule: every source in the contract needs a config entry. Every config entry needs a rationale in Part 3.
A source without a rationale is tech without strategy. A rationale without a config is strategy without execution.
props.sparkLine: Part 3 is the spec. brand_config.json is the implementation.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 3. The data contract has three fields per source: what the data IS — file type, record count, update frequency, local path; what it REPRESENTS — plain-English meaning for the user's problem, not the schema; and how we know it's good — a checkable quality standard, one per source, specific enough to be a failing test. 'The data is good' is not a standard. Traps: re-describing the file instead of the meaning, quality standards that are not checkable, skipping the local path. This contract feeds Part 4, Part 5, and eventually brand_config.json in 5B. One entry per A3 source. Get the meaning and the standard right.

props.artifactTitle: The Data Contract: What Your Inputs Actually Mean
artifactLines: 3 fields per source: what it IS (file type · count · frequency · local path).
What it REPRESENTS: plain-English meaning for the user's problem — not the schema.
Quality standard: a checkable rule — specific enough to write a failing test for it.
Trap 1: re-describing the file instead of the meaning. Read it aloud — would a non-technical person understand the WHY?
Trap 2: 'the data is good' — not checkable, not a standard.
This feeds Part 4 (validation gate), Part 5 (GIGO definition), and brand_config.json in 5B.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here are my A3 data sources: [paste your A3 source list — name, file type, record count, local path]. For each source, write a Part 3 data contract entry with three fields. Field 1 — what it IS: file type, record count or volume estimate, update frequency, and local repository path. Field 2 — what it REPRESENTS: a one-to-two sentence plain-English explanation of what this data means for the user's problem — not the schema, the meaning. Connect it to the problem statement: [paste your Part 1 sentence]. Field 3 — quality standard: one checkable rule per source, specific enough that I could write a passing or failing test. Do not write 'the data is good' or 'the data is clean' — write a measurable condition. That generates a first draft of Part 3. After it runs: does each 'represents' sentence connect to the Part 1 problem? Does each quality standard name a specific, checkable condition? If not, revise before moving to Part 4.

props.segment: The Data Contract: What Your Inputs Actually Mean
props.command: Here are my A3 data sources: [paste name/type/count/path]. For each source, write a Part 3 entry: (1) what it IS — file type, count, frequency, path; (2) what it REPRESENTS — plain-English meaning for the user's problem [paste Part 1 sentence]; (3) quality standard — one checkable rule specific enough to write a failing test for. Not 'the data is good' — a measurable condition.
props.runningText: paste your A3 source list and Part 1 sentence before running — the meaning only exists in context…

beats.narration_text: The Data Contract: What Your Inputs Actually Mean. Liam, in for Bear.
